doc.title: Issue bonds between interest dates
doc.event: Marek Co.'s {face}, {statedRateAnnual} bonds are dated January 1 (coupons July 1 / January 1) but are not sold until March 1, at par plus accrued interest. Prepare the March 1 issuance entry and the July 1 interest entry.

variants.label: Sold at par on March 1, dated January 1

entries.caption: March 1 — issue dated bonds plus accrued interest

lines.label: face + 2 months accrued
lines.why: The buyer prepays the interest that accrued before they owned the bond. On July 1 they receive the FULL six-month coupon, so the two months they didn't hold gets collected from them now.
lines.trap: Don't shorten the July 1 coupon instead — the coupon is contractual and always pays the full period. The fix happens at issuance, in cash.

lines.label: face
lines.why: Face, as always — the accrued interest never touches the bond's carrying amount.
lines.trap: Accrued interest is not part of the bond's proceeds for premium/discount purposes.

lines.label: face × stated × 2/12
lines.why: The prepaid two months parks in Interest Payable. When the full coupon is paid July 1, this payable absorbs its share and only four months hit expense.
lines.trap: Crediting Interest Expense here also works mechanically (some texts do), but the payable method keeps the logic visible — either way, only 4 months of expense belongs to the issuer this period.

entries.caption: July 1 — first coupon after a between-dates issuance

lines.label: 4 months only
lines.why: The issuer only had the money for four months (March–June), so only four months of cost is expense.
lines.trap: Expensing the full six-month coupon double-counts the two months the buyer prepaid.

lines.label: clear the 2 months
lines.why: The prepaid accrual set up at issuance is cleared when the full coupon goes out.
lines.trap: Forget this debit and your expense mysteriously balloons to six months.

lines.label: full 6-month coupon
lines.why: The contract pays whoever holds the bond the full coupon, regardless of when they bought.
lines.trap: Cash out is always the full contractual coupon — never prorate the payment itself.

computationPaths.narration: Two months of interest accrued between the dated date (Jan 1) and the sale (Mar 1). The buyer fronts it in cash; the issuer parks it in Interest Payable; the July 1 coupon then splits 4 months to expense and 2 months to clearing the payable.

memorize.body: Why the buyer prepays: coupons can't be prorated per holder — the July 1 check is one full payment to whoever holds the bond. Collecting the missed months in cash at purchase squares everyone up.
Accrued interest collected at issuance is NEVER part of the bond's issue price — it doesn't create or change a premium or discount.

questions.prompt: Bonds dated Jan 1 are sold at par on Mar 1 (semiannual coupons Jul 1 / Jan 1). How much interest EXPENSE does the issuer record for the period ending Jul 1?

distractors.feedback: That's the full coupon paid in cash. Two of those six months were prepaid by the buyer at issuance — only the four months the issuer actually had the money is expense.
Two months is the ACCRUED interest collected at issuance — the piece that is NOT expense.

traces.label: Accrued at sale ↔ payable ↔ first coupon split

build.scaffold: Timeline first: dated Jan 1, sold Mar 1, coupon Jul 1. Count the months in each slice — 2 accrued (buyer prepays), 4 owned (issuer's expense), 6 paid (full coupon). Every number is face × stated × slice/12.
